games.explanation: Cease has the shiny strikeout rate and the reputation, but the underlying profile against lefties is a lot messier than the box score suggests, and Tampa happens to run out the bats built to exploit exactly that. Aranda in particular has been a different hitter against righties this year, posting a .410 xwOBA with barrel rates that would make anyone's command wobble, and Yandy behind him keeps the pressure on. Martinez is not going to overpower Toronto, but he does not have to when the run environment projects as a coin flip and the roof takes weather out of the equation. Getting +131 on the side our numbers make a slight favorite is the entire argument, and it does not need dressing up.

Nobody wants to touch the White Sox against deGrom, which is precisely why the number got fat enough to matter. The thing is, deGrom's peripherals grade closer to very good than untouchable, and the Chicago lefties who scare him most (Vargas, Montgomery) happen to be sitting on a barrel rate north of 11% against right-handed pitching. Fedde is nobody's idea of a stopper, but a Texas lineup leaning on Pederson and Jung isn't exactly a buzzsaw either, and the simulated final lands inside a run. At +145 on a coin flip the market has priced as a rout, you're getting paid to notice that deGrom's name is doing most of the work on this line.

Laying -160 on a team that projects for less than a half-run of separation feels expensive until you notice which lineup is walking into the better matchup. Springs has been quietly hittable against righties all year, and Arizona happens to run out a top four of Marte, Perdomo, Carroll and Moreno that treats right-on-lefty at-bats like batting practice. Marte in particular has been a problem regardless of handedness, sitting near a .390 xwOBA with barrels to spare, and the Athletics lineup behind Jacob Wilson's empty average does not answer back. The market is pricing the favorite; the model is pricing the mismatch, and there's a seven-point gap between the two.

Getting +131 on the side our numbers make a slight favorite is the entire argument, and it does not need dressing up.

Atlanta is a slight favorite here mostly on reputation, because the actual game on paper is close to a coin flip between two flawed rotations. Bryce Elder gets punished by right-handed bats in a way his overall line hides, and San Diego happens to run Tatis, Machado, and Merrill at the top against him. The wind blowing in at 12 mph knocks the ceiling off any Olson-led rally, which is exactly the environment where a one-run game stays a one-run game. Getting a plus-money price on the underdog not losing by multiple runs, in a park-neutral game the sim has inside a half-run, is the whole ballgame.
